issue_title: NPE in TransportPreviewDatafeedAction when time field is absent from field capabilities response
issue_body:
## Summary

`TransportPreviewDatafeedAction.isDateNanos()` throws a `NullPointerException` when the datafeed's configured `time_field` is not present in the `FieldCapabilitiesResponse`.

## Root Cause

In `TransportPreviewDatafeedAction.java` (line 211), the code calls:

```java
fieldCapsResponse.getField(timeField).containsKey(DateFieldMapper.DATE_NANOS_CONTENT_TYPE)
```

`FieldCapabilitiesResponse.getField()` returns `null` when the requested field is not found (e.g. the field doesn't exist in the target index, or the index is empty/unmapped). Calling `.containsKey(...)` on `null` immediately throws:

```
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "java.util.Map.containsKey(Object)"
    because the return value of "org.elasticsearch.action.fieldcaps.FieldCapabilitiesResponse.getField(String)" is null
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.ml.action.TransportPreviewDatafeedAction.lambda$isDateNanos$8(TransportPreviewDatafeedAction.java:211)
```

## Fix

Add a null-check before calling `containsKey`. If the field is absent from the response, it is not a `date_nanos` field, so the method should return `false`:

```java
Map<String, FieldCapabilities> fieldTypes = fieldCapsResponse.getField(timeField);
l.onResponse(fieldTypes != null && fieldTypes.containsKey(DateFieldMapper.DATE_NANOS_CONTENT_TYPE));
```

## Impact

The preview datafeed API (`GET /_ml/datafeeds/<id>/_preview`) fails with a 500 error instead of returning a meaningful result or a user-facing validation error whenever the configured `time_field` is not yet mapped in the target indices.


Relates ES-14297
commit_message:
[ML] Fix NPE when preview datafeed checks date_nanos for an unmapped time field (#144909)

`TransportPreviewDatafeedAction.isDateNanos` called `FieldCapabilitiesResponse.getField(timeField).containsKey(...)`. When the configured time field is missing from the field capabilities response (unmapped or absent in the target indices), `getField` returns `null`, so the preview API returned 500 instead of continuing safely. The handler now treats a missing entry as “not `date_nanos`” by null-checking the map before `containsKey`, consistent with other ML code that uses field caps.

A YAML REST test previews a datafeed whose `time_field` does not exist on a mapped-only index and asserts an empty preview, so this path cannot regress to an NPE again.

Fixes https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/144888

ES-14297 #done

issue_title: [Inference API] Data URI format should be enforced for image inputs with embedding task
issue_body: The embedding task API requires that for inputs with `"type": "image"` and `"format": "base64"`, the `value` field must be a data URI, complete with MIME type prefix, but the inference API does not currently enforce this requirement. A validation check should be added to make sure that `base64` inputs match the data URI schema; i.e. that they begin with `data:<content>/<type>;base64,`
commit_message:
Validate base64 embedding inputs use data URI format (#145038)

The EMBEDDING task type requires that image inputs using the BASE64
format are data URIs. This commit adds validation that inputs using the
BASE64 format conform to the data URI format; i.e. that they begin
with "data:{some text}/{some other text};base64,"

This validation does not guarantee that the specified MIME type is
valid, that the MIME type matches the base64 data, or that the base46
data in the input is valid.

### Elasticsearch Version

main

### Installed Plugins

_No response_

### Java Version

_bundled_

### OS Version

all

### Problem Description

The query
```
ROW x=1 | STATS s=SUM(null) | EVAL s+1
```
results in a verification exception
```
line 1:11: Output has changed from [[s{r}#17, s+1{r}#19]] to [[s{r}#17, s+1{r}#19]]. """
```

I've poked around a bit. Some observations:
- even though both outputs look the same, the type of `s+1` of the left is `DOUBLE`, and on the right it's `LONG`
- I think it has to do with the surrogate of `Sum(foldable)` -> `MvSum(foldable) * Count(*)`, together with some optimizer rules that process nulls (`FoldNull`, `PropagateEvalFoldables`)

### Steps to Reproduce

execute query

_No response_

commit_message:
ESQL: Fix test bug (#145053)

Fix an old test bug that fails on rare seeds. The test creates 0 driver
which the driver runner hates. It needs to run at least one driver. In
production we run at least one driver. This updates the test to run at
least one driver. And it makes the test failure more obvious.

Closes #145001

issue_title: [Inference API] Review Update API's use of unmodifiable maps
issue_body:
The Inference API's update REST logic parses the body of the request into a few unmodifiable maps. These maps are then passed through various *Settings classes to provide the new values for the fields the user is trying to change.

This [PR](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/144787) changes this behavior to pass in a copy of these maps which are modifiable. This attempts to avoid bugs when new functionality is added but we forget to wrap the passed in map in a modifiable map (otherwise we'll get an UnsupportedOperation exception when trying to call `remove()` on the map during parsing).

It's not clear whether we need the initially parsed maps to be unmodifiable in the first place as it complicates things.

This issue is to review the logic [here](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/blob/main/x-pack/plugin/core/src/main/java/org/elasticsearch/xpack/core/inference/action/UpdateInferenceModelAction.java#L186-L187) and determine if we can provide modifiable maps from the beginning and avoid having to do a deep copy at all.

Conversation here: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/144787#discussion_r2995113022

issue_title: TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader passes unescaped bytes to FieldsVisitor causing IOOBE when _tsid hash starts with 0xFF
issue_body:
## Bug

`TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.document()` passes raw synthetic `_id` bytes directly to `visitor.binaryField()`:

```java
var uid = docValuesHolder.docSyntheticId(docID);
visitor.binaryField(fieldInfo, uid.bytes);
```

`FieldsVisitor.binaryField()` then calls `Uid.decodeId(value, 0, value.length)` which inspects the first byte to determine the encoding:
- `0xFF` → UTF-8 path (`decodeUtf8Id`)
- `0xFE` → Numeric path
- `<= 0xFD` → Base64 path

The synthetic `_id` starts with the `_tsid` bytes, whose first byte is a MurmurHash3 value that can be **any byte from `0x00` to `0xFF`**. When it happens to be `0xFF`, `Uid.decodeId` incorrectly treats the raw binary as UTF-8 encoded text. `UnicodeUtil.UTF8toUTF16` then encounters invalid multi-byte UTF-8 sequences near the end of the byte array and reads past the array boundary.

This is a format mismatch: `FieldsVisitor.binaryField` expects bytes in `Uid.encodeId` format (which escapes leading bytes `>= 0xFD` with a `0xFD` prefix), but `TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader` provides raw unescaped bytes.

## Reproduction

Minimal unit test:

```java
// Simulates the raw synthetic _id bytes that TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader
// passes to FieldsVisitor. First byte 0xFF (from a _tsid hash) triggers the
// UTF-8 decoding path in Uid.decodeId, which crashes because the remaining
// bytes are not valid UTF-8.
byte[] rawSyntheticId = new byte[] {
    (byte) 0xFF, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x07,
    0x08, 0x09, 0x0A, 0x0B, 0x0C, 0x0D, 0x0E, 0x0F,
    0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x01,  // timestamp bytes
    0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x01                              // routing hash bytes
};
Uid.decodeId(rawSyntheticId, 0, rawSyntheticId.length);
// → ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
```

End-to-end: run `TS <data_stream> | METRICS_INFO` on a TSDB data stream where some time series happen to have a `_tsid` whose MurmurHash3 first byte is `0xFF` (~1/256 chance per unique time series).

## Impact

- Any operation that triggers stored field loading on TSDB indices with synthetic `_id` can fail — this includes ES|QL commands like `METRICS_INFO` and `TS_INFO`, and potentially search operations that load source
- The bug is **data-dependent**: only triggers when the `_tsid` hash starts with `0xFF` (~0.4% of time series)
- Some data streams work fine while others fail, depending on their dimension values

## Stack trace

```
java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 28 out of bounds for length 28
    at org.apache.lucene.util.UnicodeUtil.UTF8toUTF16(UnicodeUtil.java:649)
    at org.apache.lucene.util.BytesRef.utf8ToString(BytesRef.java:139)
    at org.elasticsearch.index.mapper.Uid.decodeUtf8Id(Uid.java:163)
    at org.elasticsearch.index.mapper.Uid.decodeId(Uid.java:197)
    at org.elasticsearch.index.fieldvisitor.FieldsVisitor.binaryField(FieldsVisitor.java:108)
    at org.elasticsearch.search.internal.FilterStoredFieldVisitor.binaryField(FilterStoredFieldVisitor.java:26)
    at o.e.index.codec.tsdb.TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.document(TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.java:89)
    at o.e.index.codec.storedfields.TSDBStoredFieldsFormat$TSDBStoredFieldsReader.document(TSDBStoredFieldsFormat.java:109)
    at o.e.index.mapper.BlockLoaderStoredFieldsFromLeafLoader.advanceIfNeeded(BlockLoaderStoredFieldsFromLeafLoader.java:39)
    at o.e.index.mapper.BlockLoaderStoredFieldsFromLeafLoader.source(BlockLoaderStoredFieldsFromLeafLoader.java:47)
    at o.e.index.mapper.TimeSeriesMetadataFieldBlockLoader$TimeSeries.read(TimeSeriesMetadataFieldBlockLoader.java:78)
```

## Root cause

`TsidExtractingIdFieldMapper.createSyntheticId()` correctly Base64-encodes the raw bytes:
```java
return Strings.BASE_64_NO_PADDING_URL_ENCODER.encodeToString(id.bytes);
```

But `TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.document()` skips this encoding and passes raw bytes to the visitor, creating a mismatch with what `Uid.decodeId` expects.

## Fix direction

In `TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.document()`, escape the raw bytes to match the `Uid.encodeId` format before passing to `visitor.binaryField()`. If the first byte is `>= 0xFD`, prepend a `0xFD` escape byte — mirroring what `Uid.encodeBase64Id()` does.
commit_message:
Synthetic id term should use Base64 escape char (#145018)

The synthetic id of a document is created in TsidExtractingIdFieldMapper#
createSyntheticId from the _tsid, @timestamp and _ts_routing_hash fields 
by compacting the raw bytes values in a byte[] array.

This array of bytes is then encoded as a Base64 String using java.util.Base64.
Encoder#encodeToString. Then this string is encoded in IdFieldMapper#
syntheticIdField(java.lang.String) with Uid#encodeId which calls 
Uid#encodeBase64Id.

This last method potentially preprend an 0xfd escape char to the BytesRef 
that will be indexed into Lucene. The documentation indicates that 98.82% 
of the times (253/256) the escape char is not added, so it is added 1.18% of 
the time.

Before this change, the TSDBSyntheticIdFieldsProducer.SyntheticIdTermsEnum 
produced a synthetic id term from the doc values fields by compacting the 
raw bytes but did not prepend the Base64 escape char. It means that an escaped 
synthetic id could have been indexed but when it is retrieved it is not exactly 
the same.

This might be bad for example when recomputing the bloom filters during 
unoptimized merges where the merge iterates over all synthetic id terms.

This commit changes the TSDBSyntheticIdStoredFieldsReader.docSyntheticId() 
method the computes the synthetic id term so that it now prepend back the 
escape char. It also fixes the unit test.

While there, the method has been changed to not allocate two byte arrays each 
time a term is synthesized, it now uses a scratch ByteRef for this.

It also addresses a TODO to avoid decoding/reencoding (Little Endian byte 
array -> Uid.decode -> String -> integer -> Big Endian byte array) the routing 
hash and just reverse the bytes from Little to Big Endian.

Closes #145083

issue_title: COUNT() fails when all FORK/UnionAll branches are bare UnresolvedRelation nodes
issue_body:
`COUNT()` (and `COUNT(*)`) in a `STATS` aggregation fails with `"Plan optimized incorrectly due
to missing attributes"` when a query has multiple branches (via subqueries or views) and **all**
branches are bare index references with no commands. When at least one branch has a command
(e.g. `WHERE`), the same `COUNT()` succeeds.

This is a subquery/FORK optimizer bug, not a view bug. Views expose it because the ViewResolver
produces equivalent subquery plans, but the failure reproduces without any views involved.

## Behaviour matrix

| Branches | `COUNT()` | `COUNT(field)` |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| `FROM idx, (FROM idx)` — UR + UR (same index) | **FAILS** | OK |
| `FROM idx_a, (FROM idx_b)` — UR + UR (different indexes) | **FAILS** | OK |
| `FROM idx, (FROM idx \| WHERE ...)` — UR + Filter(UR) | OK | OK |

The bug is not related to duplicate index names — it triggers whenever **all** branches are bare
`UnresolvedRelation` nodes with no commands, regardless of whether the indexes are the same or
different.

## Error message

```
Found 2 problems
line 1:1: Plan [Project[[<no-fields>{r$}#77]]] optimized incorrectly due to missing attributes in subplans: [[<no-fields>{r$}#77]]
line 1:17: Plan [Project[[<no-fields>{r$}#77]]] optimized incorrectly due to missing attributes in subplans: [[<no-fields>{r$}#77]]
```

The `<no-fields>` reference is the internal representation of `COUNT()` which does not reference any
concrete field. When all UnionAll branches are bare `UnresolvedRelation` nodes (no commands that
produce explicit output attributes), the optimizer cannot resolve this reference in the subplans.

## Reproduction

Using the `employees` test data from the csv-spec test fixtures
(`x-pack/plugin/esql/qa/testFixtures/src/main/resources/data/employees.csv`, 100 rows):

### Failing query — same index in both branches

```esql
FROM employees, (FROM employees)
| STATS count=COUNT()
```

Expected result: `count = 200` (100 rows from each branch).

Actual result: `IllegalStateException` with the error above.

### Failing query — different indexes in both branches

```esql
FROM airports, (FROM employees)
| STATS count=COUNT()
```

Expected result: `count = 991` (891 airports + 100 employees).

Actual result: same `IllegalStateException`. The bug does not depend on duplicate index names.

### Passing query (one branch has a predicate)

```esql
FROM employees, (FROM employees | WHERE is_rehired == true)
| STATS count=COUNT()
```

Result: `count = 111` (100 + 11 rehired employees). This succeeds because the `WHERE` command
gives the subquery branch explicit output attributes that the optimizer can resolve.

### Workaround

Use `COUNT(field)` instead of `COUNT()`:

```esql
FROM employees, (FROM employees)
| STATS count=COUNT(emp_no)
```

Result: `count = 200`. This works because `emp_no` is a concrete field resolvable in all subplans.

## How to run

```bash
# Integration test that reproduces the failure (subquery, no views):
./gradlew :x-pack:plugin:esql:internalClusterTest \
  --tests "org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.CsvIT" \
  -Dtests.method="test {csv-spec:views.subqueryCountStarWithDuplicateIndex}" \
  --configuration-cache

# The test is currently commented out in views.csv-spec.
# To run it, uncomment the subqueryCountStarWithDuplicateIndex test block.
```

## Root cause analysis

`COUNT()` uses a synthetic `<no-fields>` attribute (`Analyzer.NO_FIELDS`) as a marker for plans
with no concrete field references. When an `EsRelation` has no requested fields, it uses
`NO_FIELDS` as its output attribute list.

`Fork.outputUnion()` explicitly **filters out** `NO_FIELDS` from the fork's output (line 135 of
`Fork.java`: `attr != NO_FIELDS.getFirst()`). This is by design — the comment in the Analyzer
confirms: "forkColumns do not contain NO_FIELD because Fork.outputUnion removes it."

The problem manifests at three levels:

1. **Logical plan consistency check** (`PlanConsistencyChecker.checkMissingForkBranch`): the branch
   output contains `<no-fields>` but the fork output doesn't → flagged as missing attribute.

2. **Physical plan consistency check** (`PlanConsistencyChecker.checkMissing`): the `ProjectExec`
   references `<no-fields>` but its input (from the physical merge) doesn't provide it → flagged
   as missing reference.

3. **Execution planner** (`LocalExecutionPlanner.planProject`): even if the consistency checks are
   bypassed, the planner can't find an input for `<no-fields>` → `UnsupportedOperationException`.

The issue is structural: the `<no-fields>` marker leaks into the `Project` above the Fork because
the Analyzer's `ResolveFork` rule doesn't handle the case where ALL branches have `NO_FIELDS` as
their only output. When at least one branch has a command (like `WHERE`), the branch produces real
output attributes, and the fork output alignment works correctly.

A fix requires changes at four layers, all of which have been prototyped and verified individually:

1. **`Fork.outputUnion`**: When all branches have only `NO_FIELDS`, return `NO_FIELDS` instead of
   empty list, so the fork output preserves the marker.

2. **`Fork.expressionsResolved`**: When fork output is `NO_FIELDS` and all branches have
   `NO_FIELDS`, return true (resolved) to prevent an infinite analyzer loop.

3. **`PlanConsistencyChecker`**: Skip `NO_FIELDS` attributes in both `checkMissingForkBranch` and
   the generic `checkMissing`, since `NO_FIELDS` is deliberately excluded from fork output when
   there are real fields.

4. **`LocalExecutionPlanner.planProject`**: Skip `NO_FIELDS` projections since they're synthetic
   markers with no corresponding data channel.

With all four changes applied, the consistency checks pass and the execution planner doesn't crash.
However, the actual query execution then hangs — the Fork branches with `NO_FIELDS` produce no
data channels, and the merge operator waits indefinitely for data. A complete fix also needs the
runtime merge/fork execution to handle the all-`NO_FIELDS` case correctly.

A `StripSubquery` rule was added to the Analyzer's "Finish Analysis" batch to remove `Subquery`
wrapper nodes after analysis is complete, since they serve no purpose at that point and were
obscuring the branch output in consistency checks.

## Impact on views

Views that are simple aliases (`FROM index`) produce bare `UnresolvedRelation` plans after
compaction. When multiple such views are combined (`FROM view_a, view_b` where both alias the same
or different indices), the resulting `UnionAll` has all-bare-UR branches, triggering this bug.
The `views.csv-spec` tests work around this by using `COUNT(emp_no)` instead of `COUNT()`.

commit_message:
Fix RootFlattenedFromKeyedFieldDataTests assuming document order after merge (#145226)

RandomIndexWriter can reorder documents during forceMerge, so tests that
assumed doc 0 = first-inserted doc would fail with certain random seeds.
Replace order-dependent assertions with order-independent Set<List<String>>
comparisons.

### Problem Description

If using a `dense_vector` field with `element_type` set to `bfloat16`, no default index options are applied.

### Steps to Reproduce

Create a `dense_vector` field with `element_type` set to `bfloat16`:

```
PUT test-index
{
  "mappings": {
    "properties": {
      "dense_vector_field": {
        "type": "dense_vector",
        "element_type": "bfloat16",
        "dims": 512
      }
    }
  }
}
```

_No response_
commit_message:
Fix dense_vector default index options when using BFLOAT16 (#145202)

There is a bug in the dense_vector field mapper where default index options will be set to null when the element type is BFLOAT16. This PR fixes that by handling BFLOAT16 as equivalent to FLOAT and adding a default index options test.

commit_message:
ESQL: make unmapped_fields="load" automatically trigger for partially mapped fields (#144228)

* Unconditionally load partially mapped keyword fields, whether they are mentioned in expressions or not. Also, always load values of partially mapped fields from the indices where they are mapped.
* Fix "optimized incorrectly" for `KEEP foo` with partially mapped fields.
* Move detection of PUNKs/PUKs to ResolveTable.
* Add temporary manual validation against usage of PUNKs in expressions to keep current behavior.
* Add ability to unmap fields to CsvTestsDataLoader.
